BOXING GUIDE

Your complete reference. Orthodox stance. Technique. Training. Programme.

Home — Mon / Wed / Fri Work — Tue / Thu Beginner to Sparring-Ready
Lesson 1

THE ORTHODOX STANCE

Everything in boxing is built on this. Get the stance right before you throw a single punch. Revisit it every session until it's unconscious.

Foot Position

  • Left foot forward, right foot back
  • Shoulder-width apart
  • Left toes point at target, right foot at 45°
  • Weight evenly balanced — 50/50
  • Both knees slightly bent

Upper Body

  • Left fist at chin height, 6 inches from face
  • Right fist guarding jaw
  • Elbows tucked in, protecting ribs
  • Chin DOWN — always tucked
  • Body turned ~45° to target
  • Lead shoulder slightly raised
Coach's note: If you're standing square-on, you're a bigger target and you can't generate hip rotation on your cross. Turn sideways. Feel uncomfortable? Good — that means it's new.
Lesson 2

FOOTWORK

Boxing is as much about your feet as your hands. Stay light. Stay in stance. Never cross your feet.

⬆

Moving Forward

Lift your left (lead) foot first. Let your rear foot follow. Maintain stance width throughout.

⬇

Moving Back

Move your right (rear) foot first. Push off your front foot. Keep your guard up as you retreat.

⬅

Moving Left

Left foot moves first in the desired direction. Right foot follows. Stay in your stance shape.

➡

Moving Right

Push off your front foot, move right (rear) foot first to the right. Maintain balance throughout.

NEVER CROSS YOUR FEET — NEVER STAND SQUARE
Agility note: Stay on the balls of your feet throughout — weight forward, never flat-footed. Lateral shuffles build explosive direction-change mechanics that transfer directly to any sport or activity.
Lesson 3

THE FOUR PUNCHES

Learn these in order. Don't move to the cross until the jab feels natural. Don't add the hook until the 1-2 is automatic.

```
1 The Jab

Left hand fires straight out. Twist wrist clockwise on extension. Snap it back immediately — this is the most important habit to build.

"Flick the jab — don't push it"
2 The Cross

Right hand, pushing off your back foot. Pivot the back foot, rotate the hip. Tilt shoulder and trunk forward in one motion. Snap back to guard.

"Hip first — power comes from the floor"
3 The Hook

Left hook. Elbow parallel to the floor — this is the check. Rotate hips left, pivot on lead foot. Forearm horizontal on impact. Return instantly.

"Elbow level — if it drops, it's wrong"
4 The Uppercut

Drive upward from the knees — not just an arm movement. Shift weight to that side, bend slightly, twist hips and torso upward. Keep guard up on the other side.

"Legs drive it — arm guides it"
```
The golden rule: After every single punch — return your hand to guard position before throwing the next one. This is the single most common beginner mistake. Drill it until it's automatic.
Lesson 4

COMBINATIONS

Start with 1-2. Only add the next combination when the previous one is automatic. Your last punch is always your power punch.

```
1-2
Jab — Cross
Your bread and butter. Week 1 priority.
▾

Quick jab to measure distance and set up your opponent. Follow immediately with a powerful cross. Move after every combo — don't stand and admire your work.

1-1-2
Double Jab — Cross
Two jabs to break their rhythm, cross finishes.
▾

Two fast jabs — the first probes, the second disrupts. Follow with the cross while they're off balance. Good for changing rhythm.

1-2-3
Jab — Cross — Hook
Week 3 onwards. The classic triple.
▾

Jab sets up the cross, cross creates the opening for the hook. The hook is your power finisher here. Stay balanced throughout — the hook requires good hip rotation.

1-2-3-2
Jab — Cross — Hook — Cross
Month 2. Four-punch flow.
▾

Extends the 1-2-3 with a finishing cross. The hook opens the guard, the final cross lands through the gap. This requires real rhythm — don't rush to this.

1-1-4
Double Jab — Uppercut
Month 2. Sneak the uppercut in.
▾

Two jabs to distract and lower the guard, then drive the uppercut up through the middle. The jabs need to make them think, then the uppercut surprises.

3-2
Hook — Cross
Month 2. Power combination.
▾

Lead with the hook to the side of the head, follow with the cross straight down the middle. The hook pulls their guard to the left — the cross comes straight through the gap.

```
Combination tip: Don't practise 6 combinations a week. Pick one. Drill it every session for a full week until your body does it without thinking. Then add the next one.
Lesson 5

BREATHING

Improper breathing kills performance faster than bad technique. Nail this early.

👃

Breathe In

Always inhale through your nose. Between combinations, reset your breath.

💨

Breathe Out

Exhale sharply through your mouth with every single punch. Short, sharp exhalations.

🔊

The Sound

Make a "shhhh" or "hiss" sound as you exhale. It sounds odd at first — it works.

Throwing a 4-punch combination = 4 separate exhalations. One breath out per punch, every time.
A boxer who can't supply their body with oxygen won't last. Good breathing gives you more power on each punch, more stamina across rounds, and better mental clarity under pressure.
Lesson 6

DEFENCE

Learn the concepts here. Your Tue/Thu work sessions are where you drill these in person — a good training partner is worth ten guides when it comes to defence. Start applying them progressively from Month 2.

🦶 Footwork

Moving in and out of range, pivoting, circling. Proper footwork is the foundation of all defence — most punches miss because of good feet, not good hands.

🤸 Head Movement

Slipping — move your head to the outside of a punch. Ducking — bend at the waist to avoid head shots. Never stay static.

🛡️ Blocking

High guard covers your head with gloves. Low guard protects the body with elbows and forearms. Parrying redirects punches with your glove.

🔄 Rolling

Bend at the waist, rotate your upper body to evade hooks and uppercuts. Particularly effective in close range. Practice this as a drill before sparring.

📐 Distance Control

Staying at the right range. Too close — can't punch. Too far — can't land. Control the space between you and your opponent at all times.

🎭 Feinting

Fake an attack to make your opponent react. Use a feint jab to get them to raise their guard, then attack the body. Month 2–3 concept — something to work on with your sparring partners at work.

Before you spar: Discuss high guard and head slipping with your MT training partner — they'll drill it with you properly. Distance control instincts are best picked up from an experienced sparring partner, not from a guide alone.
Lesson 7

TRAINING METHODS

Each tool serves a different purpose. Understand what you're training when you use each one.

👤
Shadow Boxing
Technique · Speed · Fluidity

Visualise an opponent. Work your combinations and defensive movements. This is more valuable than most people realise — you're building muscle memory with no resistance, which means you can focus entirely on perfecting form. Do 3 x 3-minute rounds every session. Rest 1 minute between rounds.


Agility focus: Move like you're creating space — short explosive bursts, change direction constantly, stay light on your feet. Shadow boxing is one of the best agility drills there is.

🥊
Heavy Bag (EDX 5ft)
Power · Stamina · Combinations

Always wrap your hands and wear gloves. Start slow — single punches, find your range and rhythm. Increase intensity as you warm up. Practice your footwork around the bag — don't stand in front of it like a statue. Move after every combination. 4 x 3-minute rounds with 1 minute rest.


Round structure: R1 — Jabs only. R2 — 1-2 combos. R3 — Freestyle. R4 — Body work (lower your level).

🎯
Pad Work
Reaction · Timing · Accuracy

One person calls and holds, the other responds and hits. Swap every 2 minutes. The pad holder must hold the pads firm and in the correct position — sloppy pad holding teaches sloppy punching.


Calling system: Use the number codes — call "1" for jab, "2" for cross, "1-2" for the combination. Build up to calling "1-2-3" once the hook is drilled. Vary the pace and sequence.


Reaction focus: React to pad calls without hesitation. The caller can also move the pads to make you track and follow them — this is one of the best reaction and agility drills in boxing.

🏃
C25K + Skipping
Aerobic Base · Boxing Cardio

C25K: Run after boxing, not before. 3 runs per week on Mon/Wed/Fri. Week 1: 60-second jog / 90-second walk × 8. Each week the running intervals extend. By Week 6–7 we transition some treadmill sessions to skipping.


Skipping (Month 2+): The single best boxing-specific cardio tool. Improves footwork, coordination, shoulder endurance, and cardiovascular fitness simultaneously. Start with 3 × 3-minute rounds, aiming for continuous rhythm rather than speed.

💪
Strength & Conditioning
Month 2 onwards

Don't add this in Month 1 — focus entirely on technique and cardio base first. From Month 2, incorporate bodyweight and compound work.


Priority exercises: Push-ups (chest, shoulders, core), bodyweight squats (lower body power for punching), plank variations (core stability), burpees (boxing conditioning). Later: medicine ball throws for explosive power.


Note: Don't overcomplicate Month 1 with strength work. Build the technique and cardio base first. Bodyweight and compound exercises from Month 2 onwards.

Your Plan

THE PROGRAMME

Mon/Wed/Fri — home training, 5pm. Tue/Thu — work sessions with your MT and boxing partners. Sunday is full rest.

Month 1

  • Stance, jab, cross — nailed
  • Hook introduced Week 3
  • C25K habit established
  • Comfortable on the bag
  • Routine locked in

Month 2

  • Hook and uppercut drilled
  • Combinations up to 1-2-3-4
  • Skipping introduced
  • Technical sparring prep at work
  • Explosive pad rounds at home

Month 3

  • Light technical sparring at work
  • Skipping replaces some runs
  • Full reaction pad rounds at home
  • Defence techniques active
  • Noticeable fitness gains

Month 4

  • Full programme in place
  • Sparring regularly at work
  • Agility and conditioning gains visible
  • Review and set new goals
  • Weight loss target well underway
🩹

Wraps. Always.

No exceptions. Wrists and knuckles from session one. Even for 5 minutes on pads.

📅

Show Up.

3 home sessions, every week. Results come from regularity, not intensity. Don't skip Fridays.

😄

Have Fun.

The technique matters. The consistency matters. But so does enjoying it.
